1: David escapes to the cave of Adullam (mĕʿāraṯ ʿăḏullām). Adullam was in the Shephelah foothills of Judah — rugged terrain offering natural fortress geography. The cave became David's first base of operations during his years as a fugitive. The Psalms associated with this period (Pss 34, 57, 142) reflect the emotional register of this flight: fear, trust, praise amid extremity. His brothers and father's household come to him — the very family Samuel bypassed when anointing the youngest son is now drawn to him in crisis.
2: Three classes of men gather to David: those in distress (māṣôq), those in debt (nōšeh), and those bitter of soul (mar nepeš). The 400-man band that forms around David is the social underside of Saul's court economy — those whose lives are not working within the existing power structure. This gathering has sociological as well as theological significance: YHWH's anointed draws the marginalized, not the established. The Psalms of lament that emerge from this period encode the spirituality of those who have lost standing.
3: David takes his father and mother to the king of Moab at Mizpah, asking that they stay under the Moabite king's protection while David waits to see what God will do. The Moab connection is unexpected until one remembers that David's great-grandmother was Ruth the Moabitess. David's appeal to Moab is a kinship appeal — he has family claim on Moabite protection. This is also an act of filial piety: David's flight does not include abandoning his aged parents to the hazards of cave life.
4: David's parents stay with the king of Moab as long as David is in the stronghold. Hammĕṣûḏāh (the stronghold) is the same term used for Masada — a fortified high place. David moves between the cave of Adullam and various mountain strongholds throughout 1 Samuel 22-26. The protection of parents during the son's military campaign is a recurring narrative detail that characterizes David's household responsibility even in extremity.
5: The prophet Gad — who will later serve as David's court prophet (2 Samuel 24:11) and co-author of records (1 Chronicles 29:29) — appears here for the first time, instructing David: 'Do not remain in the stronghold; go into the land of Judah.' The divine word through a prophet redirects David back into the territory of his own tribe. This is both strategically and theologically significant: David must be in Judah, in covenant territory, rather than in the security of a cave. Gad's appearance establishes that David continues to receive prophetic guidance throughout the fugitive years.
6: Saul hears that David and his men have been discovered. He sits at Gibeah under the tamarisk tree on the hill, spear in hand, surrounded by his servants. The tamarisk tree (ʾešel) is a specific detail — possibly a throne-tree, the kind under which kings held court (cf. 1 Samuel 31:13 where Saul is buried under the tamarisk). The spear in Saul's hand is a recurring image: it is the weapon he twice hurled at David (1 Samuel 18:11; 1 Samuel 19:10) and at Jonathan (1 Samuel 20:33). The symbol of power has become a symbol of paranoia.
7: Saul addresses his Benjaminite servants: 'Will the son of Jesse give every one of you fields and vineyards? Will he make you all commanders of thousands and commanders of hundreds?' Saul's appeal is explicitly tribal and economic: he frames David's potential kingship as a threat to Benjaminite patronage networks. The language of fields, vineyards, and military ranks recalls Samuel's warning about royal extraction (1 Samuel 8:14-16). Saul wields the fear of losing what the king gives as a loyalty mechanism.
8: Saul's paranoid accusation: 'that all of you have conspired against me; that none of you discloses to me when my son makes a league with the son of Jesse.' He uses the covenant vocabulary of Jonathan and David's relationship (kāraṯ bərît, make a covenant) but frames it as treason. Saul's inability to distinguish covenant friendship from political conspiracy is a symptom of the evil spirit that has replaced the Spirit of YHWH. His claim that 'none of you is sorry for me' (ḥālaṯ ʿālay) reflects the isolation of a man who has rejected the bonds that would make loyalty voluntary.
9: Doeg the Edomite, the man who 'was detained before YHWH' at the Nob sanctuary (1 Samuel 21:7), now speaks: 'I saw the son of Jesse coming to Nob, to Ahimelech son of Ahitub.' The informer is an Edomite — a foreigner in Saul's service, and the only one willing to exploit Ahimelech's hospitality. Doeg's identity as an Edomite gives him no stake in Israelite covenant relationships; he is the outsider who sees opportunity in Saul's paranoia. Psalm 52 is attributed to this moment.
10: Doeg reports that Ahimelech inquired of YHWH for David, gave him provisions, and gave him the sword of Goliath. The three acts — oracle consultation, bread, weapon — are precisely what was needed for a fugitive on a mission. The sword of Goliath kept at the sanctuary as a trophy now becomes evidence of collusion. Doeg's testimony is selective: he does not mention Ahimelech's question about David's status (1 Samuel 21:2) or his apparent belief that David was on a royal mission.
11: Saul summons Ahimelech and all his father's house — the entire priestly community of Nob — to appear before him. The summoning of the full priestly family indicates Saul intends a judgment against the whole community, not just the individual. Nob was the sanctuary city that housed the displaced priesthood after Shiloh's destruction; it was the institutional home of the Elide priestly line that Samuel had already pronounced under judgment (1 Samuel 2:31-36).
12: Saul addresses Ahimelech: 'Listen now, son of Ahitub.' The formal address by patronymic signals judicial procedure. Ahimelech answers: 'Here I am, my lord.' His posture throughout the interrogation is calm and transparent — he does not attempt to deny or obscure what happened, because he acted in good faith.
13: Saul's accusation: 'Why have you conspired against me, you and the son of Jesse, by giving him bread and a sword and by inquiring of God for him, so that he has rebelled against me and lies in wait for me?' Saul reframes every act of priestly hospitality as conspiracy and reframes David's flight as armed rebellion. The inversion is complete: the one pursued becomes the pursuer, the priestly act of service becomes treason.
14: Ahimelech's defense is direct: 'Who among all your servants is as faithful as David? He is the king's son-in-law, and the captain of your bodyguard, and honored in your house.' Ahimelech argues from David's known status — he had every reason to believe David was on legitimate royal business. The defense appeals to David's demonstrated covenant loyalty, his family ties to the king, and his military rank. All of these were true — and all of them are what Saul had already dismantled through jealousy.
15: 'Was it the first time I inquired of God for him? By no means! Let not the king impute anything to his servant or to all the house of my father, for your servant has known nothing of all this, little or great.' Ahimelech insists on his ignorance of any political dimension. His defense is factually strong: he had consulted YHWH for David before (on legitimate royal missions) and had no reason to suspect a change in David's status. The phrase 'little or great' (qāṭān wəgāḏôl) means 'in any measure whatsoever' — a comprehensive denial.
16: Saul pronounces the death sentence: 'You shall surely die, Ahimelech, you and all your father's house.' The judgment is collective — the entire priestly family is condemned for one man's hospitality, rendered in good faith, to the king's own son-in-law. The injustice is stark: no counter-evidence is allowed, no witnesses are called, no process is observed. Saul's paranoia has wholly displaced the judicial function of kingship.
17: Saul commands his guards to kill the priests of YHWH. But none of his servants will stretch out their hand to strike the priests of YHWH — kî lōʾ ʾāḇû yāḏām liślōaḥ lipgôaʿ bəḵōhănê YHWH. The refusal of Saul's own Israelite soldiers is the narrative's moral landmark: even the king's sworn servants recognize a line they will not cross. The inviolability of YHWH's priests was so fundamental that the soldiers' conscience overrides direct royal command. Saul's order is a line Israel will not follow.
18: Saul turns to Doeg: 'You turn and strike the priests.' Doeg the Edomite turns and strikes the priests — 85 men who wore the linen ephod. The specific number and the detail of the priestly vestment (ʾēpôḏ bāḏ) makes this an official priestly execution — these were consecrated servants killed in their sacred office. Doeg has no scruple about killing the priests of a covenant he does not share. The Edomite does what Israel's soldiers refused to do.
19: Doeg strikes Nob, the city of priests, with the sword — men and women, children and infants, oxen, donkeys, and sheep — the total destruction formula of ḥērem (ban). The slaughter of Nob is the inversion of the holy war command: the ḥērem was prescribed against Canaanite idolaters and the Amalekites, but Saul applied it to the city of YHWH's own priests. The Agag chapter (1 Samuel 15:7-9) showed Saul unwilling to apply ḥērem to the commanded target; here he applies it to the covenant people themselves. The irony is the narrator's indictment.
20: One son of Ahimelech son of Ahitub escapes: Abiathar, who flees to David. The survival of Abiathar fulfills the prophecy of 2:33 that 'all the increase of your house shall die by the sword of men' yet 'one man shall not be cut off from my altar.' Abiathar is the remnant of the Elide priestly line — the single survivor who carries the priestly tradition and the ephod into David's camp.
21: Abiathar reports to David that Saul has killed the priests of YHWH. The news is not surprising to David — his foreknowledge of Doeg's danger was already expressed in 1 Samuel 21:7. But learning it has happened is different from suspecting it might. The priestly massacre becomes the event that binds Abiathar and David together for the rest of David's life; Abiathar will remain David's priest until Adonijah's ill-fated bid (1 Kings 2:26-27).
22: David says to Abiathar: 'I knew on that day, when Doeg the Edomite was there, that he would surely tell Saul. I have brought about the death of all the persons of your father's house.' David's confession is remarkable: he accepts moral responsibility for the massacre without having intended it. His knowledge of Doeg's presence, combined with his choice to involve Ahimelech anyway, makes him a causal agent. David does not deflect the weight of this onto Saul alone.
23: 'Stay with me; do not be afraid, for he who seeks your life seeks my life. With me you shall be in safekeeping.' David's protection-promise to Abiathar is covenant language: he takes the priest under his personal protection in full acknowledgment of his own partial responsibility. The phrase 'with me you shall be safeguarded' (kî mišmeret ʾattāh ʿimmî) uses the language of the sanctuary watch — David becomes Abiathar's refuge. The priest who has lost everything finds covering under the king who lost everything too.
